title: Confirmation Bias: Why You Believe What You Want to Believe
titleAr: انحياز التأكيد: لماذا تصدق ما تريد تصديقه
learningObjective: After this exercise, the user will be able to define confirmation bias (Nickerson, 1998), recognize it in their own information-seeking behavior, and apply deliberate disconfirmation seeking as a counter-strategy with measurable improvement (target: d=0.4 per Kenyon, 2014).
learningObjectiveAr: بعد هذا التمرين، سيكون المستخدم قادرًا على تعريف انحياز التأكيد (نيكرسون، 1998)، والتعرف عليه في سلوكه الخاص في البحث عن المعلومات، وتطبيق البحث المتعمد عن الدحض كاستراتيجية مضادة.

content.scenario:
Confirmation bias (Nickerson, 1998) is the tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information that confirms your existing beliefs. Kahan (2017) calls the related phenomenon 'motivated reasoning' — where your conclusions are driven by your identity and values rather than evidence.

Scenario: You believe strongly that social media is harmful to teenagers' mental health. You search online for information about this topic.

Here are 6 search results. Notice which ones you are naturally drawn to vs. which ones you want to skip. That attraction/avoidance pattern IS your confirmation bias in action.
content.scenarioAr: انحياز التأكيد (نيكرسون، 1998) هو الميل للبحث عن المعلومات التي تؤكد معتقداتك الحالية وتفسيرها وتذكرها. يسمي كاهان (2017) الظاهرة ذات الصلة 'التفكير المحفز' — حيث تكون استنتاجاتك مدفوعة بهويتك وقيمك بدلاً من الأدلة.

task.instructions: For each search result, identify whether it CONFIRMS your belief (social media is harmful) or CHALLENGES it (evidence is more nuanced). Then select ALL results you should read for a balanced understanding.
task.instructionsAr: لكل نتيجة بحث، حدد ما إذا كانت تؤكد اعتقادك أو تتحداه. ثم اختر جميع النتائج التي يجب قراءتها للحصول على فهم متوازن.

items.text: 'Social media linked to depression in teens, major study finds' — Nature (2023). This CONFIRMS your belief. You should still read it, but recognize why it feels satisfying.
items.explanation: CONFIRMING — This feels right because it matches your belief. The bias trap: you might accept this uncritically because it's what you expect. Even confirming evidence should be scrutinized.

items.text: 'The relationship between social media and teen mental health is more complex than headlines suggest' — APA Monitor (2023). This CHALLENGES your belief.
items.explanation: CHALLENGING — This is the result your confirmation bias wants you to SKIP. Kenyon (2014) found that deliberately reading counter-attitudinal evidence is the single strongest debiasing strategy.

items.text: 'Smartphones destroy a generation' — The Atlantic (2017). This strongly CONFIRMS your belief with emotional language.
items.explanation: CONFIRMING + EMOTIONAL — This uses loaded language ('destroy') that triggers both confirmation bias AND emotional appeal. Double the reason to pause and verify.

items.text: 'Large-scale study finds social media effects on wellbeing are trivially small' — Orben & Przybylski, PNAS (2019). This CHALLENGES your belief with data.
items.explanation: CHALLENGING — Orben & Przybylski (2019) found that social media use explains only 0.4% of variance in teen wellbeing — comparable to wearing glasses or eating potatoes. This data-driven challenge is exactly what confirmation bias makes you want to dismiss.

feedback.correct: Excellent! You correctly identified that ALL results should be read, especially the ones that challenge your belief. Nickerson (1998) showed this is extremely hard — your brain literally rewards confirming information with dopamine. The counter-strategy: deliberately seek out the strongest argument against what you believe.
feedback.correctAr: ممتاز! لقد حددت بشكل صحيح أنه يجب قراءة جميع النتائج، خاصة تلك التي تتحدى اعتقادك. أظهر نيكرسون (1998) أن هذا صعب للغاية — دماغك يكافئ حرفياً المعلومات المؤكدة بالدوبامين.
feedback.incorrect: Did you skip the results that challenged your belief? That's confirmation bias in action. The key principle: you should be MOST interested in evidence that contradicts what you already believe.
feedback.incorrectAr: هل تخطيت النتائج التي تحدت اعتقادك؟ هذا هو انحياز التأكيد في العمل. المبدأ الأساسي: يجب أن تكون أكثر اهتمامًا بالأدلة التي تناقض ما تعتقده بالفعل.

whatNotToDo: Don't assume that evidence confirming your belief is automatically stronger or more credible than evidence challenging it
Don't use the phrase 'I already know this is true' as a reason to stop checking — that's confirmation bias speaking
evidence: Nickerson, R. S. (1998). Confirmation bias: A ubiquitous phenomenon in many guises. Review of General Psychology, 2(2), 175-220.; Kenyon, T. (2014). False polarization: Debiasing as applied social epistemology. Synthese, 191(11), 2529-2547.
safetyNote: This exercise uses social media and mental health as an example topic. For concerns about your own social media use, consult a qualified mental health professional.
com_b_mechanism: Confirmation bias awareness enables deliberate counter-attitudinal evidence seeking
